title: SEGMENT 15 — The spin-offs: TCGA, GTEx, UK Biobank, the omics revolution

lines.text: SEGMENT 15 — The spin-offs: TCGA, GTEx, UK Biobank, the omics revolution
We have walked through the history of the reference genome itself, from 2003 through T2T and the pangenome.

Now I want to step back and talk about the spin-off effects — the projects and infrastructures that exist because HGP existed, and that have arguably had larger practical impact than the reference sequence itself.
The Cancer Genome Atlas, TCGA, was launched in 2006 as a joint NHGRI and National Cancer Institute project.

The idea was simple in concept and enormous in execution: take thousands of tumors, sequence the cancer genomes, sequence the matched normal tissue from the same patients, and catalogue the somatic mutations that drive cancer.

Over its life, TCGA characterized more than eleven thousand tumors across thirty-three cancer types, producing petabytes of genomic, transcriptomic, methylation, and clinical data, all of it freely available.
TCGA changed cancer biology.

It established that almost every cancer is genetically distinct at the molecular level, that most cancers are driven by a small number of recurrent driver mutations layered over many passenger mutations, that cancers can be classified more meaningfully by their molecular profile than by their tissue of origin, and that targeted therapies designed against specific driver mutations can produce dramatic clinical responses.
The modern era of precision oncology — sequencing a patient's tumor to choose their treatment — is built directly on TCGA infrastructure.
The Genotype-Tissue Expression project, GTEx, launched in 2010, took the same logic in a different direction.
GTEx collected matched samples from up to fifty-four tissue types in nearly a thousand deceased donors and measured gene expression — what genes are turned on at what levels — in every tissue.
The result is the definitive atlas of where in the body every human gene is expressed.
If you want to know whether a gene is on in liver, in brain, in pancreas, in testis — GTEx tells you.

Combined with the variation catalogues from 1000 Genomes, GTEx allowed researchers to map expression quantitative trait loci, eQTLs — genetic variants that affect how much of a particular gene is expressed — across the human genome.
The UK Biobank is perhaps the most consequential single resource produced in the post-HGP era.

Launched in 2006, the UK Biobank recruited five hundred thousand participants aged forty to sixty-nine, collected baseline health data and biological samples, linked the participants to their electronic health records over decades of follow-up, and then — beginning in 2017 — genotyped all five hundred thousand on dense SNP arrays, and beginning in the early 2020s, generated whole-exome and whole-genome sequencing on the entire cohort.
The UK Biobank is the largest publicly available genotype-phenotype dataset in the world.
Researchers anywhere on earth can apply for access, and within a few months they can be running analyses on data from half a million people linked to comprehensive lifetime medical records.
The number of papers using UK Biobank data is now in the tens of thousands and growing exponentially.
Other biobanks are now coming online at similar or larger scales.
The All of Us Research Program in the U.
S. is enrolling a million participants with explicit emphasis on demographic diversity.
The FinnGen project in Finland leverages that country's exceptional population registry data.
The China Kadoorie Biobank covers half a million Chinese participants.
Japan's Tohoku Medical Megabank covers regions affected by the 2011 tsunami.
The point is this.
The Human Genome Project produced a reference.
The reference enabled a set of techniques — SNP genotyping arrays, then short-read sequencing, then long-read sequencing — to be applied at scale.
Those techniques enabled population-scale biobanks.
The biobanks are where modern human genetics actually lives.
We are now in the era where the unit of analysis is not one genome but a million genomes, linked to a million sets of medical records, analyzed across decades.
None of that exists without HGP.
The whole edifice rests on the foundation poured between 1990 and 2003.
